narrative: As an Asian male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some academic areas, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 48% chance of failing the bar exam on your first try, tipping the decision toward rejection to prioritize students with stronger projected success. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of past students' academic records, family backgrounds, and exam outcomes, focusing on who reliably passes the bar right away. The model's decision hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence: your undergraduate GPA, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2 (well below the average 5.53), and your LSAT score of 22.70 (far under the typical 36.70). Your strong undergrad GPA was the most powerful positive force, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass by showing you have the college-level academic chops that often carry over to law success. In contrast, your lower Year 3 decile ranking had a notable pull in the opposite direction, signaling to the model that your performance relative to peers slipped in later undergrad years, a common red flag for bar struggles among similar students. Your LSAT score reinforced that concern, as lower scores like yours compared to the norm often correlate with challenges on the bar's rigorous format. While factors like your middle family income bracket and full-time student status (shared by over 90% of applicants) played smaller roles, they didn't outweigh these top influences. Compared to typical admitted students, who often cluster in higher deciles and boast LSATs above 35, your profile aligns more with those who've faced bar hurdles, even with your GPA strength. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male applicant—many in your demographic excel—but the model weighs these metrics conservatively to minimize risks for the program. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your later-year grades through post-bac courses or targeted prep, and reapplying with updated transcripts that highlight upward trends. You might also seek feedback from admissions counselors on bolstering weaker areas or explore bridge programs that support students with profiles like yours. With focused effort on these, your solid GPA foundation could position you strongly for approval next cycle.
